description: For classroom-tech briefs and digital-tool rollouts with a calm SaaS-style structure.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "clean SaaS/edtech explainer infographic" in a modern, print-friendly style with a white background and plenty of whitespace, a clear grid, and soft pastel-colored sections. At the top, place a large centered headline in dark sans-serif (bold) across two lines, with clear hierarchy and high readability. In the middle, place a central "hub" illustration: a large simple table/spreadsheet icon (rectangular grid) in light grey with subtle shadow, and above it a cluster of small flat document icons (colored cards, a book, paper) "flying in" toward the hub with a soft, transparent triangle/cone that suggests downward flow. Around the hub, place four equally prominent audience panels in a 2x2 arrangement (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right), where each panel has its own soft pastel background with rounded corners and a light drop shadow (blue, yellow, red/pink, green) plus a small icon in the upper left (flat/semi-flat vector with simple contour) followed by a bold headline row and a short description line. Inside each panel, include a clear miniature table (grid with column headers and 3–4 rows) with thin lines, a light header background, and slightly rounded corners so the tables look consistent and "UI"-clean. Behind the hub, place a very subtle four-petal shape in transparent pastels matching the panel colors, so the center feels unified without distracting from the text. Typography should be consistent sans-serif: headings in bold, body in regular, table text small but crisp; icons should be clean and simple with mild gradients and discreet shadows, no hard black outlines. The color palette should be modern and soft (pastel blue, pastel yellow, pastel red/coral, pastel green) with dark grey text and thin grey lines in the tables; avoid neon, dark HUD, vintage paper, hand-drawn sketches, and photorealism — the goal is a scannable, professional marketing/product infographic that explains a concept via a central hub and four use-case panels with table examples.

title: Style 44
description: For three-option briefs — tool choices, routines, or lesson models — at a glance.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "clean product explainer infographic" in a modern edtech/SaaS style with a white background, plenty of air, and a clear grid. At the top, a large centered main headline in dark navy bold sans-serif should dominate, with a smaller grey subtitle on one line below. Under the headline, place three large, equally wide column cards side by side (1–3) with rounded corners, soft drop shadows, and subtle gradient backgrounds, each with its own color tone (left blue, middle orange, right green); each card should have a clear colored top strip with step number and heading in white text, and a simple icon-based illustration in the middle (flat/semi-flat vector with light shadow and soft gradients, consistent stroke weight) plus 3–5 small icons/labels around a central "hub" icon (e.g. a brain/circle) that shows categories; at the bottom of each card, a short summary sentence in dark text. Along the bottom across the full width, include a separate "Pro Tip" banner in purple/lavender with rounded corners, a large bold headline on the left and 1–2 lines of explanatory text, plus on the right a row of 2–3 small icon badges with short labels beneath (same icon style as above). Typography should be consistent sans-serif with clear hierarchy (large title, medium card titles, small labels), and the design should feel like a professional marketing slide: clean margins, even spacing, no hand-drawn lines, and no photorealistic imagery. The color palette should be soft and modern (navy text, pastel blue/orange/green panels, lavender pro-tip), with subtle gradients and discreet shadows but no neon or HUD glow; avoid vintage paper, grunge, heavy outlines, and messy collage layouts — the goal is a scannable, print-friendly, "Notion/Canva/SaaS" explainer with three step cards and a pro-tip banner at the bottom.

title: Style 45
description: For 2x2 comparison handouts used to frame options in a staff meeting.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal minimalist "editorial comparison card" infographic with lots of whitespace and a soft, modern museum/textbook feel. The background should be warm off-white/ivory with subtle paper grain and no hard framing. At the top, place a large centered heading in elegant serif (book/essay feel) alone with generous margins, followed by a 2x2 grid of four equal quadrants separated by thin light grey divider lines (a cross). Each quadrant should have a soft, transparent color gradient filling the background (pastel yellow, warm coral/pink, teal/blue-green, purple) at low opacity so the text remains very legible; the gradients should be soft and diffuse, without hard edges. In each quadrant, include a simple line icon in the same tonality (e.g. sun, moon, globe/star) placed to the left of the heading, drawn as a thin outline with slightly rounded corners. The heading in each quadrant should be in uppercase or small caps in dark grey/black serif or clean sans-serif (consistent), followed by 2–3 short lines of body copy in a smaller, legible font, and at the bottom a small bullet row (a dot + short phrase) as an "Observances:"-style label; everything left-aligned with clear typographic hierarchy and even line spacing. The color palette should be muted and academic: dark grey text, light grey lines, soft pastel fields; no illustrations beyond the icons, no photos, no heavy frames, no shadows that feel like UI cards. The overall impression should be a clean, calm editorial infographic with four comparative fields, subtle gradient blocks, thin icons, and an elegant serif title, without neon, without hand-drawn sketches, and without technical HUD aesthetics.

title: Style 46
description: For calm, organised handouts — tool comparisons, lesson kit lists, and routines at a glance.
prompt: This design should convey a well-organized, pedagogical, and harmonious feel through a balanced and professional appearance. The visual identity is built on a background vertically split into two equal halves, where the primary color is a pastel pink shade with color code #F6D0D6, complemented by a light blue accent color in #CBE4F0 and secondary tones of yellow #FDF6DA, pure white #FFFFFF, and neutral grey #9EA4A9. The image style consists of photorealistic office supplies arranged in a strict 'knolling' composition seen straight from above, where analog tools are mixed with technical devices in a clean, spacious room. Objects are placed in a grid-like pattern with even spacing along a central vertical axis, creating a symmetrical balance that guides the viewer's eye in a natural flow from left to right as a metaphor for the journey from problem to solution. Textures are detailed with a focus on matte paper, smooth plastic housings, and a metallic luster on rulers, all supported by a soft, diffuse overhead light that generates gentle, realistic cast shadows. Typography consists of a modern sans-serif in Japanese Gothic style with medium weight for headings, while body copy stays in a clean dark grey sans-serif with normal weight. Information labels and tags are placed contextually and directly on objects, for example on digital screens or sticky notes, to maintain the minimalist and structured impression.

title: Style 47
description: For myth-versus-reality handouts in media-literacy, AI, or digital-wellbeing units.
prompt: Create a modern, easily scannable two-column infographic in a "Myth vs Reality" style with a friendly, pedagogical tone. The layout should be landscape (16:9) and built as an even grid with several horizontal rows (5–7). Composition: At the top, a clear main headline in bold sans-serif, centered. Left column: heading "MYTH" (or equivalent) and each row starts with a short label "MYTH:" followed by 1–2 lines of text. Right column: heading "REALITY" and each row starts with "REALITY:" followed by 1–2 lines of text. In the middle: a vertical, soft divider that looks like a lightly translucent "curtain/veil" or wave form with a subtle gradient, separating the columns. Row modules: Each row should have its own pastel-colored background stripe (striped rows) harmonized across the whole design. In each row: place a small vignette illustration (2D cartoon scene or icon cluster) on the right of each column. Illustration style: 2D semi-flat vector with soft gradients, rounded forms, discreet shadows, no hard black outlines. Simplified people/figures and clear prop icons, consistent style throughout the image. The left side can have a soft "sky/cloud" feel in the background (subtle). Color palette: Pastels — light blue, teal, mint, warm yellow, coral/orange, light green. High readability: text in dark grey/black, clean surfaces, plenty of air. Typography: Sans-serif throughout, clear hierarchy (heading largest, labels bolder, body smaller). Short copy, scannable and social-media-friendly. Negative instructions: Avoid photorealism, 3D render, neon/HUD style, vintage paper, grunge, thick black outlines, over-detailed characters, cluttered layout, long paragraphs.

title: Style 48
description: For clear onboarding flows — new routines, digital platforms, or year-start guides.
prompt: This design should be infused with a professional and hopeful tone that feels both informative and light through a distinctly airy manner. The graphic profile rests on a background in a fresh, pale blue tone (#F4F9FB) where text in a muted grey-blue shade (#667882) and a clear accent color in sky blue (#5DB6E3) create a clean contrast, supported by secondary colors of darker slate blue (#3B6075), ash grey (#91AAB8), and pure white (#FFFFFF). The imagery is characterized by a fully flat vector style with a central, S-shaped timeline that softly guides the eye downward along a vertical axis. The composition is built on a layout with generous whitespace where information alternates between left- and right-aligned orientation around the winding spine, reinforced by simple geometric figures and discreet dashed guide lines. The texture is consistently matte and smooth to mimic a digital vector surface with paper-like simplicity under a uniform, shadowless, bright lighting. The iconography consists of flat symbols in two tones, sky blue and dark slate, while typography uses tall condensed sans-serif headings, a well-crafted geometric sans-serif for body text, and bold dates clearly marked in the accent color.

title: Style 49
description: For data-driven briefings to principals when a technical isometric diagram carries the argument.
prompt: This design profile should convey an analytical and professional feel through a pedagogical and well-structured expression. The visual base consists of a very light grey background color (#F9F9F9) contrasted by a dark grey text shade (#222222) and a clear blue accent color (#5B9BD5), where deep red (#C0392B), light sky blue (#EAF4FC), and various greyscale shades in #888888, #FFFFFF, and #D3D3D3 are also used to create clarity. The image style is built on technical line art with isometric diagrams, architectural symbols, and dimensional markers presented with schematic cleanliness on a surface reminiscent of smooth digital paper. The composition is arranged in a comparative split-screen layout with a vertical hierarchical flow and generous whitespace, where all elements, including floor-plan outlines and isometric stairwells, are strictly aligned to a grid. Lighting is completely flat and diagrammatic with no color gradients to ensure consistent visibility. Details are drawn with thin, precise 1–2 pixel lines, while logical flows are marked with dashed lines; active parts receive a blue fill and errors or transitions are indicated with red dashed arrows. Typographically, a powerful sans-serif in Gothic style or Helvetica is used for the large main headlines and section titles, while body copy stays in a legible medium weight. For technical specifications and labels, a condensed sans-serif or a fixed-width typeface is used, creating a clear hierarchy that runs from the top all the way down to the smallest annotations.

title: Style 50
description: For Bauhaus-inspired art, design, or maths lessons built from primary shapes.
prompt: This design should be infused with an analytical and structural tone with a sense of modern retro that is distinctly instructional in its expression. The visual identity rests on a background in a softly creamy shade (#F4F1E8), where text is kept in a deep dark grey tone (#2D2D2D) and a red accent color (#D94646) brings the composition to life alongside secondary tones of blue (#2C65A6) and yellow (#F2C044). The illustration style is characterized by overlapping geometric primitives such as circles, triangles, and squares, where a "multiply" effect lets the colors blend and create new shades where the layers meet, reinforcing a subtle risograph texture with matte paper finish and a hint of ink bleed in the transparent fields. The composition is built on a dynamic diagonal that leads the viewer's eye from the lower-left corner toward the upper-right through a well-balanced asymmetry and modular information clusters. Lighting is entirely flat and depth is created solely through the transparency of the colors and the interplay of the layers rather than through traditional shadows, where thin line-drawn icons mix with solid shapes and are connected by fine arrows indicating a clear process flow. Typographically, a powerful sans-serif in Gothic style is used for headings and a very legible sans-serif in medium weight for body text, where the visual hierarchy is controlled through clear size differences and numbered sequences while text blocks are left-aligned and anchored directly to the geometric elements.

title: Style 51
description: For travel, languages, and global-citizenship units with a passport-like textured feel.
prompt: This design should radiate an adventurous but official tone through an aesthetic that is both inviting and tactilely textured, almost like a luxurious government document. The visual base consists of a creamy background tone in color code #F5F2E9, where the dark main text (#2D2D2D) is joined by a vivid coral accent (#FF7F50) and complementary touches of deep pink (#E84A5F) and muted sage green (#88B085). The imagery mimics a passport spread filled with authentic details such as ink stamps, visa decals, and barcode elements, where the surface is characterized by a matte paper texture, porous ink bleed, and subtle fiber noise along worn edges. The composition is asymmetrically divided with an anchoring object on the left and a clear typographic hierarchy on the right, all lit by a soft, scanned light that creates a bright, shadowless atmosphere. Overlapping round and square stamps in various colors create layers of depth, while the main heading is given a warm linear gradient from orange to pink. Typographically, a powerful geometric sans-serif for headings is combined with a clean, legible humanist sans-serif for body text, while numerical data and technical details are presented in a fixed-width typewriter style to reinforce the feel of official paperwork.

title: Style 52
description: For science and PE units where a single dramatic visual has to carry the lesson.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "premium dark science anatomy poster" in a National Geographic/IMAX documentary style with a black to very dark brown background, subtle vignette, and light film grain. The main motif should be a dynamic, photorealistic action silhouette in motion, rendered as an advanced "x-ray/cutaway composite": semi-transparent skin with a glowing skeleton (white/bone-colored) and clearly visible muscles/organs in warm red/orange tones, as if a medical rendering were overlaid on a realistic body; use strong rim light along the contours and a slight neon-like glow around bones and organs (controlled, not HUD), plus small dust/particle trails around paws for a sense of speed. Around the body, place many thin white annotation lines that point to specific parts, with small headlines in orange and short descriptive text in white/light grey sans-serif; keep the typographic hierarchy clear: a large headline in uppercase at the top left, a short standfirst below, and then multiple callouts on the right and left. At the very top, a thin decorative sequence strip with small silhouettes in a row should show a "motion progression" (a series of outlined positions) in light grey/orange as a graphic timeline. At the bottom, place 1–2 small data visualizations in editorial style: a horizontal bar/ranking chart with thin lines and labels, plus a timeline/scale bar with tick marks and numbers, all in muted colors (white/grey with orange accent) and very fine stroke weight. The color palette should be strict and dramatic: black/dark brown background, bone-white skeleton glow, warm red/orange organ/muscle accents, and white/grey text; avoid flat vector icons, pastels, hand-drawn sketches, and glassmorphism — the goal is a highly polished, cinematic, scientific anatomy poster with a transparent body, glowing skeleton/organs, many callout lines, a large title, and small charts at the bottom.

title: Style 53
description: For ecology and biology posters with a nature-documentary broadcast look.
prompt: Create a "National Geographic Wild event poster infographic" in a dark, cinematic nature-documentary style with a deep rainforest/jungle background (dark green, high contrast, subtle mist, and leaf texture) and a clear TV-channel/brand feel. At the very top, place a wide green banner with white text (event title + broadcast info) and a small logo box in one corner; typography should be clean sans-serif and feel like broadcast graphics. Below the banner, place a large main headline in modern sans-serif, left-aligned, with a thin kicker line in smaller text below, and build the whole poster as an information-rich but luxuriously structured layout with many small fact boxes. In the middle, a large, dramatic 3D "low-poly" globe/planet should dominate, with faceted geometry and hard lighting, surrounded by a ring of city/environment silhouettes and several animals in realistic photo-collage/3D-hybrid style climbing, sitting, or moving around the globe; use strong rim light and warm accent lights (yellow/orange) on the globe's edges to create glow, plus small sparks/particles for energy. Around the globe, place several modular information panels in a grid (left column, right column, and a panel or two at the bottom), each with a short heading row, 1–2 sentences of body copy in white/light grey, and a large number in warm yellow that stands out (statistic feel); the panels should be bounded by thin yellow lines, small corner markers, and minimal iconography (simple silhouettes, small diagrams, small map markers) in cyan/teal as accents. Add thin connector lines that point from certain panels toward the globe or toward small animals, to create an "annotated poster" feeling. The color palette should be consistent: dark jungle green and black as the base, white text, yellow/gold for numbers and lines, and cyan/teal for small icons; avoid pastels and light backgrounds. The overall impression should be a high-contrast premium documentary infographic with a broadcast banner, a central faceted globe with animals, and many fact boxes with large yellow numbers and thin yellow-framed lines, without hand-drawn style and without modern glassmorphism — more like a Nat Geo poster with cinematic photo/3D hybrid and a clear editorial data layout.

title: Style 54
description: For ambitious science spreads that pair a painted scene with dense data on the facing page.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "National Geographic double-page spread" in premium editorial natural-science style with two clear pages: the left page as a full-page storytelling illustration with a large vertical headline and standfirst, and the right page as a dense, data-rich infographic based on the theme of the sources. The imagery should be natural-history painting in high detail (digital gouache/watercolor + realistic texture), with a warm savanna/earth palette (sand, ochre, beige, muted green) and soft atmospheric haze; avoid strong neon colors and keep everything like a printed magazine. Left page: a dramatic, realistically painted scene with a large subject tailored to the sources in the foreground and a hint of the relevant theme in the background, with depth and soft sunlight; place a large headline in black serif/sans with strong typographic presence and a short body-copy column below, plus small discreet captions and a thin baseline with footer/page number as on a printed spread. Right page: a large "hero" profile illustration of the same theme in the middle. Place several small inset diagrams around the centered image in rectangles with thin frames and arrows showing motion, a small map/range inset in a corner, and a small "timeline/range" box with an iconic map shape; use discreet accent colors (yellow for arrows/markers) but keep the text in dark grey/black. Along the bottom of the right page, place a row of small sequence illustrations showing steps in a process, plus a small legend/key; keep them very fine and print-quality. Typography should be strictly editorial: headlines in strong serif/sans, body copy in small serif with tight leading, uppercase section heads, exact margins and alignment; the whole piece should feel like a December issue of a science-magazine feature with realistic nature painting, overlays, and many small explanatory insets, without modern UI glassmorphism and without hand-drawn sketchnote.

title: Style 55
description: For psychology, health, or neuroscience lessons built around a reflective portrait.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "editorial science magazine infographic" layout in a National Geographic-like style with a photorealistic, softly graded background and overlaid medical diagrams. The background should be a large, atmospheric, high-resolution photographic image in muted blue-grey tones (soft contrast, slight film grain) showing a calm human face in profile/half-profile with eyes closed, and over the face an anatomical cross-section illustration of the brain should be superimposed as a semi-transparent, carefully rendered medical rendering (soft pink/beige tissue tones, clear structures, thin label lines), so that photo and anatomy merge into a "double exposure" feel. Place a large left-aligned headline in elegant serif (magazine typography) with a short standfirst in smaller serif below, and keep the margins generous like a printed spread. Place 2–3 round "inset" circles (callout bubbles) over the image showing magnifications/diagrams in clinical illustration style (light background, technical arrows, color-coded markings in muted orange/blue), with thin connector lines that point to relevant areas in the main image; the circles should have a fine white border and subtle shadow to feel like inset figures. On the right, place a column with short bullet paragraphs/labels in sans-serif or light serif, with small subheadings and thin lines organizing the information; use discreet color accents for keywords (e.g. muted orange/blue) but keep the majority of the text in dark grey for high readability. At the bottom, place small data visualizations in editorial style (a simple timeline chart with thin lines and small labels plus 2–3 small colored brain/heatmap circles), arranged as a row of figures with captions. The whole design should feel like a serious science feature: sophisticated typography, photo-based background, medical overlays, thin annotation lines, subtle shadows, and print feel; avoid flat vector, neon/HUD, hand-drawn sketchnote, and overly colorful icons — the goal is a premium editorial infographic with photo + anatomical overlay + circular inset diagrams and small, elegant data graphics.

title: Style 56
description: For marine-biology and anatomy explainers that need quiet editorial depth.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "premium science magazine infographic" in a National Geographic-like editorial style with a photorealistic underwater background in a deep ocean-blue gradient (light rays from above, subtle particle haze, soft vignetting) and a main motif that is a large, translucent marine organism/structure rendered photorealistically in high detail, where parts of the body are shown as an elegant cutaway/section with semi-transparent layers. The layout should be strictly editorial and technical: thin white/grey annotation lines, dotted leader lines, small callout points, discreet arrows, and micro-text that identify subcomponents; use color-coded flow lines (e.g. cyan, yellow, magenta, green) that show internal process flows through the cutaway, with clear but subtle arrows and a legend feel without heavy frames. Combine several small "inset" diagrams in various shapes (circles and rectangles) with minimalist schemes, sequence steps, and simplified icons, placed along the center line and lower half to explain mechanisms; let the insets have transparent backgrounds or faintly tinted panels so they blend into the water. Typography should be modern and sober: large title in thin, wide sans-serif or elegant semi-serif lower left, with a short standfirst in smaller text, plus several section headings in uppercase with clear hierarchy; all text should be white or light grey with high readability against the blue background and with consistent column structure as in a printed spread. Use a timeline/sequence row at the bottom with small icon illustrations in simple line style (white/light blue) and small captions, plus a small "family tree/classification"-like mini-graph in fine line (very subtle) to give an encyclopedic feel. The imagery should feel scientific and calm: realistic materials (gel/transparency), soft highlights, controlled contrast, no neon-HUD effects, no hand-drawn sketch lines, and no modern app cards; the whole should read as a professional, print-ready, ocean-based science infographic with photorealism, cutaway visualization, thin annotation lines, color-coded flows, and several small explanatory inset diagrams in a well-balanced editorial layout.

title: Style 57
description: For watercolour-style nature studies where annotations hang off a single beautiful subject.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "premium ocean science magazine infographic" in a National Geographic-like editorial style with a cool, misty-blue background gradient and subtle ocean-depth grain (light noise, soft vignetting). The main motif should be a large, ultra-detailed illustration dominating the upper half, rendered in realistic watercolor/gouache + fine natural-history drawing style (lots of textural detail, soft shadows), and on the illustration, small discreet blue marker dots should be connected by thin lines to small inset bubbles (circular magnifications) that show close-ups of structure/material in the same realistic style. The layout should be strictly editorial: title in the upper left in elegant serif uppercase, short standfirst, and several small text blocks in columns with clear subheadings, all in white or light grey text with high readability; use thin leader lines, small arrows, and minimal icon markers as in a printed science feature. In the lower half, place 2–3 large circular map/information insets with thin outlines and subtle sheen, where each circle has a simplified map/diagram surface with several color-coded route lines (red, blue, purple) and small dot markers; place short captions beside each circle and keep the map graphics clean and print-ready (no neon, no thick lines). On the right side, place 2–3 rectangular "concept panels" in isometric/diagram style with simple arrows and wind/flow lines, showing principles with small silhouettes and soft shadows, all in the same cool blue palette with white/light grey linework. Typography should be consistent magazine: headlines in serif, body in legible serif/sans with tight leading, small figure numbers, and discreet legends; use lots of air, exact alignment, and a clear hierarchy. The color palette should be muted and scientific: blue-grey background, natural neutral tones in the main illustration, and limited accent colors only for route lines and markers; avoid flat vector, hand-drawn sketchnote, glassmorphism, and HUD glow — the goal is a print-ready, high-end natural-science infographic with a large realistic main motif, thin annotation lines, circular map insets with color-coded tracks, and small technical diagram boxes in a clean editorial layout.

title: Style 58
description: For geography units that weave map, diagram, and 3D render into one coherent spread.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "National Geographic-style explanatory science spread" with a cool, muted color palette and a blend of cartography, technical diagrams, and photorealistic 3D visualization. The background should be light grey-blue with subtle paper texture and plenty of air; typography should be editorial: a large left-aligned headline in serif/sober display, followed by a short standfirst in smaller text, and clear subheads in uppercase/sans-serif for sections. The layout should be divided into three levels: at the top left, a compact map/overview in flat cartography style with soft color blocks and thin lines; at the top right, a large detailed map image with dense thin trail lines/stroke patterns and a discreet legend; and at the bottom, a dominant photorealistic 3D diagrammatic visualization in isometric perspective showing an atmospheric phenomenon as a volumetric cloud formation with realistic mist, soft lighting, and clear motion/flow arrows. On the 3D visualization, include several thin annotation lines, label boxes, and arrows in two accent colors (e.g. red for upward flow and blue for downward) plus some grey arrows for overall direction; text labels should be small, technical, and consistently placed with thin lines pointing to exact parts. Along the middle or lower left, place a row of numbered steps (1–4) as small headings with short explanations, arranged in a straight line as a process explanation; use small icon markers or numbers in circles. Also include a small photo inset at the lower right (rectangle with thin white frame) that looks like a documentary close-up, and a discreet figure text/credit line at the very bottom as on a printed magazine page. The whole design should feel like a serious science infographic: exact cartography, realistic volumetric 3D scene, thin annotation lines, controlled accent colors, high readability, and a strict grid; avoid neon, glassmorphism, hand-drawn sketches, and flat cartoon icons — the goal is a print-ready, editorial "magazine spread" with map + map + large 3D process visualization and clear technical callouts.

title: Style 59
description: For literature and history spreads where a portrait headline sets the tone.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "National Geographic-style feature infographic spread" with strong editorial contrast where the upper left has a deep black background with a large dramatic headline in high-contrast serif display (white uppercase, newspaper feel) and the rest of the page is clean white with a strict grid, thin lines, and scientific diagrams. Let a large, ultra-detailed main motif dominate the right half: a semi-realistic digital painting of the subject in profile with very rich texture and clear color variation, lit with a soft studio feel and subtle shadow on the white surface; use fine contours and realistic structure but keep the expression as illustrated natural science rather than photo. Around the main motif, place thin grey annotation lines pointing to specific parts with small labels in sans-serif, and a few short text blocks in columns with headings in uppercase, all in sober editorial typography. On the left side in the white canvas, place an elegant process/evolution section with small stylized shape icons in pastel colors (cyan, pink, purple, green) connected with thin lines showing a progression, like a scientific "evolution/sequence" but without subject-specific words. At the very bottom, let a large, very clean phylogenetic/timeline-like diagram strip run horizontally across the full width: thin grey baselines, many color-coded branches in soft accent colors, small dot markers and short labels, plus 2–3 smaller illustrations placed on top of the timeline as "reference figures" with their own small captions; everything should be extremely fine-lined and print-ready. Use a limited but sophisticated accent palette: black/white as the base, grey annotation lines, and muted but clear colors only in diagram lines and small icons; keep margins generous and alignment exact like a magazine spread. Avoid neon/HUD, glassmorphism, and hand-drawn sketchnote; the goal is a premium editorial science infographic with a black headline corner, a large main motif on white, thin callouts, and a large fine-line timeline/cladogram at the bottom with color-coded branches.

title: Style 60
description: For biology and zoology lessons that treat the animal as the hero of the page.
prompt: Create a "National Geographic-style wildlife anatomy poster" in a premium editorial natural-illustration style with a painterly, misty-green rainforest/jungle background (soft brushstrokes, slight grain, atmospheric haze) and a large, realistic main subject dominating the center: an animal depicted in half-profile with spread wings/limbs, rendered as natural-history illustration in watercolor/gouache + fine detail drawing (dense feather/fur structure, correct proportions, soft shadows). Integrate a clear anatomical "cutaway/overlay" on part of the body where muscle/tendon/structure is highlighted via a transparent cross-section surface in warm red/orange tones, with thin white/grey annotation lines and small labels pointing precisely to joints and tendons; use dot anchors and discreet arrows to show direction of motion. Place a large headline in elegant serif in the upper right with a short fact column of text below (magazine layout), and add a small inset map in one corner (a small world map/regional map in muted colors) with a marked range area, plus a small "diagram inset" in another corner (e.g. a simple top view/sound or form sketch) drawn as thin line graphics with a short caption. Along the bottom, include small comparison icons/silhouettes and a mini scale (small animal silhouettes and measurement notations) in monochrome or faint yellow, plus 1–2 small close-up insets of specific structures (e.g. claws/bones) in rectangles with thin frames and short captions. Typography should be consistently editorial: headlines in serif, body in small sans/serif with high readability, and key accent words in discreet yellow/ochre; use thin separators and plenty of air around text blocks. The color palette should feel NatGeo: jungle-green background, neutral natural tones on the animal, white/light grey text and lines, with sparing ochre/yellow accents for arrows and highlights; avoid neon, flat vector, hand-drawn sketchnote, and glassmorphism — the goal is a print-ready, museum-quality natural-science infographic with a painterly animal portrait, anatomical overlay, thin callouts, small inset maps, and comparative silhouettes in a sophisticated magazine layout.

title: Style 61
description: For ocean and ecosystem units told as a painted panorama with scientific captions.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "premium ocean science magazine spread" in a National Geographic-like editorial style with a photorealistic underwater environment in a cold blue palette: a large panoramic scene where the water surface appears as a light horizon line at the top, with misty mountains/clouds in the distance above the surface and a deep, clear blue underwater space below with subtle light rays, particles in the water, and soft vignetting. The main motif should be a dynamic, photorealistic hunting/behavior scene with several marine animals in motion at varying sizes and depths (foreground sharp, background slightly hazy), where a large school/"bait ball"-like cluster of small fish forms a massive textured formation that the larger animals circle around; use realistic motion, bubbles, and water swirls to suggest energy. Overlay thin, discreet annotation lines and callouts pointing to specific parts of the scene, with small white dot anchors and short labels in light sans-serif; keep the text in several small columns along the upper part of the image with clear subheads and short paragraphs, like a magazine infographic with generous margins. Include a large headline in the lower left in elegant serif (white/light grey) with a short standfirst below, and keep the rest of the body copy in smaller, clean sans-serif with tight but legible leading. Place a small circular inset in the upper right showing a simplified "zoom" view or mini-diagram (thin white border, subtle shadow), plus a clear sequence row at the bottom with 4–6 small rectangular step panels showing a simplified "frame-by-frame" progression in miniature (small images on blue background with thin frames and small numbers/labels), like a storyboard/analysis strip. The color palette should be strictly ocean-blue (cyan → teal → deep navy) with white typography and very sparing accent colors (e.g. light yellow for isolated markers/arrows), and the whole expression should be serious and editorial: photorealistic nature-documentary feel, subtle film grain, no neon/HUD effects, no hand-drawn sketches, and no flat vector icons — the goal is a print-ready, high-end science infographic where a photorealistic underwater scene carries the narrative, complemented by thin callouts, columned text, a circular inset, and a small sequence strip at the bottom.

title: Style 62
description: For climate-change explainers built around a single fotorealistic geology slice.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "National Geographic-style climate science spread" with a clean white background and a blend of photorealistic 3D geology, technical cutaways, and editorial typography. The layout should feel like a printed magazine spread: a large left-aligned headline in elegant serif, a short standfirst in smaller text, and several small text columns with subheads in uppercase/sans-serif; use generous margins, a strict grid, and plenty of whitespace. The main motif should be a large photorealistic 3D-rendered landmass/terrain slice in perspective (like a cut-out model) with a semi-transparent "ice/layer" cap on top, where one large corner is folded/lifted like a paper flap to reveal the layers underneath (cutaway/peel-back effect); show clear material contrast between ice (light cyan, translucent) and bedrock (dark stone, textured), with soft shadows and realistic lighting. On the left, place a tall rectangular vertical cross-section illustration (a technical 3D cube/column) that shows layers descending in a block model with thin label lines and small labels, like a geological core sample; use light blue tones for ice/water and neutral greys for rock, with very fine contour lines. Spread thin annotation lines (grey) that point from text to specific points on the 3D model and the cross-section column, with small dot anchors and discreet arrows; keep labels small and precise. Along the bottom, place a band-like section with several small mini-graphics and micro-diagrams in a row (e.g. small map insets, a simple area chart/legend, small timeline steps), all with thin frames, small figure numbers, and short captions, like a "figure strip" on a magazine page. The color palette should be muted and scientific: white background, black/dark grey text, light cyan/ice blue for ice elements, neutral grey/brown for rock, and very sparing accent colors for markings; avoid neon, glassmorphism, hand-drawn sketchnote, and flat cartoon icons — the goal is a sober, photorealistic, editorial science infographic with a 3D cutaway terrain, a vertical cross-section column, thin annotation lines, and a bottom edge with small figures and captions in a strict magazine layout.

title: Style 63
description: For current-affairs briefings where a heatmap of Europe tells the whole story.
prompt: Create an infographic with "breaking news/geopolitics" imagery: a high-contrast, super-saturated, simplified map of Europe filling the entire image, with clear country borders in light, thin contour lines and a slight 3D/relief feel on the ground surface; use a dramatic heatmap color coding across countries/regions (extreme red for "hot zone", deep navy for "cold zone", and neutral areas in yellow/green), with soft gradient transitions and strong color saturation; add a subtle vignette and "clarity/sharpen" look that makes edges crisp and colors pop. Over the map: 2–3 large, thick, curved arrows (white with dark outline/soft shadow) showing direction/consequence, placed so that they lead the eye from one hotspot to other areas; the arrows should feel like a clear visual "hot take" annotation, simple and readable even at mobile size. Text block: upper left, a black rectangle with rounded or hard corners (thumbnail style), with large, heavy, condensed uppercase sans; a two-line headline where the keyword is marked in yellow and the rest in white (high contrast), with light drop shadow/outline for maximum readability; keep the text short, aggressive, and clickbait in style, but without extra subheads. Composition: the text block should balance the map and the arrows; hotspots should dominate visually in red; everything should feel like a modern "geopolitical explainer" with maximum clarity, strong colors, hard contrasts, minimal noise, no small legends, no watermarks, no logos, and no fine print.

title: Style 64
description: For classic chalkboard-style summaries when a lesson should feel like old-school teaching.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal infographic that looks like a photographed classic green chalkboard with a clear chalk texture, light dust/smears, faint vignetting, and a subtle perspective as if the shot was taken straight on but with minimal lens distortion. Use a "chalkboard lecture slide" aesthetic: headlines in large, warm gold/ochre letters that look like chalk (slightly grainy edges), body copy in white chalk with clear bullet lists and even indents, plus strategic emphasis in pink/magenta chalk (underlines, small marks, highlighted words) and the occasional accent in light green chalk for variation. The layout should be strictly structured like a teaching poster: a large main headline centered at the top, then four distinct text blocks placed in quadrants (upper left, upper right, lower left, lower right) separated by thin chalk lines or a cross-shaped grid, where each block has a yellow/ochre subheading and 3–5 bullets in white chalk. In the middle, place a chalk-drawn simple diagram figure in "white chalk line art" style (e.g. three overlapping circles/Venn or a simple schematic) with small labels in white chalk and a short center text, plus a few hand-drawn chalk arrows around the diagram pointing between the blocks to create flow. Also include small hand-drawn chalk icons (e.g. an arrow, a small marker, a simple symbol) that feel quickly sketched by a teacher, and make sure everything has authentic chalk lines with uneven thickness, small breaks in the strokes, and a light "chalk dust" halo around important headlines and diagrams. Keep the color palette limited to chalkboard green background + white chalk + yellow/ochre headings + pink/magenta emphases (and possibly very sparing light green), avoid clean digital vector lines, glossy 3D, neon/HUD, minimalist corporate style, or paper texture — it should feel like a real chalkboard with a pedagogical lecture layout and clear readability at a distance.

title: Style 65
description: For warm classroom summaries that still feel like something a teacher drew by hand.
prompt: This design should be infused with a pedagogical and inviting tone that feels both informative and nostalgically structured, much like a classic but modern school blackboard. The visual identity rests on a dark background in slate grey (#263238) where off-white text (#F5F5F5) and a glowing yellow accent color (#F4D03F) create clear readability, complemented by a lively palette in red, light blue, purple, and orange. All imagery should have the character of hand-drawn chalk illustrations, complete with decorative banners, small doodle embellishments, and schematic arrows that tie the information together in clearly framed boxes. The surface should feel like a matte chalkboard with visible chalk dust and raw, grainy lines that appear to have been created with varying pressure from the hand. The composition follows a centered vertical flow where the various information blocks are bordered by hand-drawn frames, lit by a flat but contrast-rich light that makes the simple, sketchy icons and their hatched shading pop against the dark surface. Typographically, a powerful handwritten style like a thick marker is used for headings and a clean, printed hand for body text, where the most important points are emphasized with extra-thick strokes, underlines, and the strong colors yellow and red to truly catch the viewer's attention.

title: Style 66
description: For study-technique lessons and journaling prompts modelled on a perfect flat-lay notebook.
prompt: Here we have a scene that breathes both diligence and order: an exclusive and high-resolution flat-lay image featuring an open, bone-white notebook resting on a solid wooden surface. The right page of the book is entirely covered with compact yet meticulously organized handwritten text, executed with a fine fountain pen and deep black ink. The handwriting is neat and tidy to fit an enormous amount of information, yet remains fully legible. The content is built with a knife-sharp visual hierarchy where a large, double-underlined main heading sits at the top, followed by numbered main sections with bold headings that make the page easy to scan. Beneath these headings, a network of deeply nested sub-points spreads out, complemented by small arrows for logical flows and clarifications in parentheses. To maximize information density, the layout includes several complex visual elements, such as a detailed three-column table filled with checkmarks and short comments, and a four-field matrix where each quadrant is densely filled with text along defined axes. The page also houses a carefully drawn circular four-step flow model and small, valuable margin notes that provide additional context to the subject. Beside the book lies a fine-nibbed fountain pen, and the whole scene is bathed in a clear, even light that ensures every tiny letter and line appears with perfect sharpness, giving the viewer the impression of a deeply studied and invaluable document.

title: Style 67
description: For workshop summaries, project-kickoff boards, and facilitator handouts with hand-drawn energy.
prompt: Create a hand-drawn whiteboard/sketchnote infographic that looks like a photo of a whiteboard (with metal frame) or flip chart. The background should be white with a light paper/whiteboard texture, subtle noise, and faint vignetting at the edges. Layout: At the top, a large hand-drawn headline in blocky uppercase (marker-pen style) and a smaller subtitle below. Divide the content into three main columns with clear large boxes: Left column: a vertical "story/arrival" sequence with 4–6 step boxes top-to-bottom (each step has a small doodle icon and 1–2 lines of text). Middle: a large central explanation box with 3–5 sub-blocks and arrows between them (incl. a clear warning box with warning triangle). Right column: 3 stacked theme boxes ("Aspect 1, Aspect 2, Aspect 3" or similar) with small icons and short bullets. Show flow with thick hand-drawn arrows from left → middle → right. At the bottom: a large cloud-shaped summary bubble with a short "takeaway" text in hand-drawn style. Style: Everything should be sketched with marker pen: uneven hand-drawn contours, small doodles, underlines, and handwritten headings. Boxes have light marker fills (pastel) and dark outlines. Icons are simple doodles (light bulb, gear, speech bubble, warning triangle, person, chart, etc.) in the same hand-drawn style. Colors: Dominant blue for the main headline and some frames. Green accent for the left column, orange accent for the right column, blue for the middle box. Small red warnings and yellow highlights. Colors should look like marker/highlighter, not digital neon. Photorealism level: Not photorealistic objects, but the whole composition should feel like a photographed whiteboard document with slight perspective and mild lighting variation. Negative instructions: Avoid perfect vector ruler-straight lines, glossy 3D, neon HUD, minimalist corporate infographic, overly clean gradients, high-resolution photorealism.

title: Style 68
description: For maths and science walk-throughs that mimic a photographed university whiteboard.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "university lecture notes" diagram image that looks like a photographed whiteboard: warm-white board surface with faint flameyness, light erase marks and marker smear, discreet noise and soft vignetting at the edges, with a minimal sense of perspective as if someone had taken a straight-on photo of the board. Use handwritten marker style throughout: headline at the top in black marker with uneven stroke weight and natural handwritten letters, and build the diagram with clear rectangular blocks and arrows drawn by hand. The composition should be two large main blocks side by side with plenty of air between them: a left block framed in blue marker and a right block framed in red marker, where each block contains 2–4 inner rectangles with headings in matching color (blue on the left, red on the right) and smaller supporting text in black; use simple hand-drawn brackets/feedback arrows inside the blocks to show loops and feedback. Place a thick hand-drawn arrow in blue running from the center of the left block to the center of the right block to show transfer, and a thick red arrow running from the side of the right block down toward a separate small box on the right, framed in black and containing a short label; in addition, place small "Nx" markings beside each main block in the same color as the block to suggest repetition. Typography should feel like lecture notes: large colored block headings (e.g. "ENCODER"/"DECODER"-like) in handwritten caps, smaller labels in black, and consistent simple iconography (only boxes, arrows, underlines, and a few wavy lines for emphasis). The color palette should be strictly limited to black, blue, and red marker on a white board, with no digital gradients, no vector-perfect lines, and no slick UI elements; everything should feel quickly but clearly drawn by a teacher, with authentic unevenness, small overlaps in pen strokes, and a light camera shadow that makes the image feel like a real photo of a whiteboard diagram.

title: Style 69
description: For numeracy step-by-steps that feel exactly like a teacher writing on the board.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal image that looks like a real photo of a whiteboard in a classroom, with a metal frame and rounded corner brackets, faint vignetting, softly uneven lighting, and clear erase marks/marker smears on the warm-white-grey board surface. The content should be handwritten in black whiteboard marker in a clean, pedagogical "teacher writing on the board" style: a large headline at the top with a short phrase and a mathematical expression line (generic, not subject-specific), followed by a numbered step-by-step list (1–5) split into two columns, where the left column has steps 1–3 and the right column has steps 4–5, with even line spacing, clear indentation, and consistent handwriting. Each step should have a short one-line instruction and beneath it 1–2 lines of symbolic "working out" text in the same handwriting, with a few simple arrows or equals signs that convey a sense of progression, but without locking in to specific content. In the lower right, include a framed result box drawn with the same pen (a rectangle with slightly uneven lines) with a short "Solutions/Result" label inside. Also add small realism details: a whiteboard eraser placed on the tray at the bottom, plus a whiteboard marker lying horizontally, both slightly blurred and photorealistically rendered with a photo feel while the text and lines are sharper; keep everything minimalist and monochrome (only black marker on whiteboard), avoid digital vector lines, perfect typefaces, colorful elements, neon, or graphic UI cards — the goal is authentic university/high-school lesson notes photographed straight on with natural unevenness in pen strokes, small shakes in lines, and subtle shadows from the frames.

title: Style 70
description: For primary-years literature and imagination projects with a soft Ghibli-inspired hand.
prompt: Overall Design Guidelines Feel and atmosphere: A nostalgic and educational aesthetic balanced by a whimsical, magical aura and a softly defined structure. Visual Identity Background tone: #FAF8F2 Text color: #2B2B2B Accent color: #212635 Complementary palette: #9ABF89, #F4B98E, #89C2D9, #E6D7C3, #5D8C57. Imagery and Style Aesthetic characteristics: Character design inspired by Ghibli characters and small, detailed character vignettes. The composition is tied together by soft, organic paths and flows. Backgrounds and environments should have a seamless watercolor wash where scenes flow into one another. Use of narrative icons in an illustrative style to reinforce the story.

title: Style 71
description: For SEL and wellbeing lessons where a doodle-style mascot carries the message.
prompt: Atmosphere and mood: The aesthetic should be playful and personal, with a feel that is both pedagogical and approachable. Visual identity — Background tone: #F4F2E9 (warm cream). Text color: #2B2B2B (dark anthracite). Accent color: #6FA3D2 (soft blue). Complementary colors: #FF9EC6 (pastel pink), #F2E66B (sunshine yellow), #8D6E54 (earth brown), #A8A8A8 (neutral gray). Imagery and style — Characteristic traits: Illustrations should have a hand-drawn look with elements of doodles and small mascots that reinforce the feeling of a personal notebook. Characters are simplified (stick-figure-like) but have expressive facial expressions. Texture and surface: The surface should simulate matte paper with visible fiber structure. Use effects that imitate felt-tip ink and the semi-transparent feel of a highlighter. Composition and light: An organic, sectioned layout where text blocks alternate with small illustrative scenes (vignettes). Lighting should be flat and even, as if the image had been scanned, without dramatic shadows. Line work: Uniform monoline ink strokes with softly rounded ends. Decorations: Sprinkle hearts, stars, sparkles, and arrows to create visual flow and a charming frame. Typography — Headlines: Hand-drawn bubble-style typefaces with diagonal hatching (stroke fill) and clear outlines. Body copy: A neat, easy-to-read handwritten style (sans-serif feel). Emphasis: Important parts are marked with pastel highlighter strokes in pink and yellow.

title: Style 72
description: For creative-writing and storybook projects inspired by Ghibli's watercolour warmth.
prompt: This design should be infused with a nostalgic and magical mood that combines the pedagogical with a whimsical, softly defined structure. The color palette rests on a background in the tone #FAF8F2, where text in #2B2B2B and accent details in #212635 create a harmonious base, complemented by the secondary shades #9ABF89, #F4B98E, #89C2D9, #E6D7C3, and #5D8C57. The imagery draws inspiration from the world of Ghibli, with character vignettes and organic paths that tie the scenes together through soft watercolor gradients and narrative icons. The surface has a matte finish that simulates a watercolor wash with a digital softness and a subtle sense of paper texture. The composition is governed by a winding narrative path in the upper portion that transitions into a dense environmental collage in the middle, before finally landing in a clearly grid-based footer. Lighting is soft and natural with a warm glow and gentle transitions entirely without sharp shadows, and the technique builds on digital watercolor with soft contours framed by organic shapes and gentle gradients. The visual flow guides the eye from left to right along a timeline with distinct thematic clusters. Typography exploits contrast in size and color-coded section headings, with headings set in a bold, all-caps humanist sans-serif, body text in a clean geometric sans-serif, and captions in a condensed sans-serif variant.

title: Style 73
description: For STEM handouts built on blueprint paper with crisp engineering cards.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal STEM infographic in clean "engineering textbook + blueprint UI" style with a light background resembling graph paper/drafting paper (faint blue-gray grid across the whole surface) and a large top banner in dark blue with a white, bold sans-serif headline in uppercase. Use a clear modular layout with rounded rectangular information cards with thin blue outlines, a light inner shadow, and plenty of air; card headlines should be in bold sans-serif with a small parenthetical/subtitle line underneath in regular sans-serif. Build a central "hub" section in the middle with a darker header plate and connect all the cards with thick, rounded flow arrows and pipe-like connectors in two accent colors (cool blue and warm orange) that weave between the panels with soft 90-degree bends and clear arrowheads, so the image feels like a schematic flow chart. The illustration language should be technical but friendly: simple 2D/semi-flat icons with a light gradient and subtle shadows (e.g. gauges, cubes, aircraft wing, hydraulics, map, organ) centered in each card; lines and symbols should be clean and consistent, without a hand-drawn feel. Include two larger side panels in the middle row showing contrasting diagram illustrations with flow lines (one "ordered" image with parallel lines and one "chaotic" with vortices), and a smaller central formula/principle box between them in a smaller card, all connected by arrows from the hub. At the bottom there should be a row of 4 equally sized application cards with their own icons, and a vertical side label on the left edge in uppercase (as a section heading) to provide textbook structure. The color palette should be blue-dominant (dark blue header, light blue outlines, blue diagram lines) with orange as a secondary accent in flow arrows and highlights; avoid neon, photorealism, heavy texture, vintage paper, and chaotic layout — the goal is a modern, print-friendly, high-resolution educational infographic that looks like a professional technical chart with grid, cards, arrows, icons, and clear hierarchy.

title: Style 74
description: For upper-secondary engineering explainers where three clean panels show cause and effect.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "clean engineering explainer" infographic in modern technical report style with a light background featuring a very subtle grid (graph paper) and a thin white frame; use a discreet top edge/gradient border in cyan → purple that gives a mild futuristic accent without dominating. At the top left, a large headline in modern sans-serif (dark gray) should sit, with a short sub-line in smaller text beneath, all airy and left-aligned. The main layout should consist of three equally wide panels in a row, separated by thin vertical lines; each panel should have a simple technical illustration in semi-3D/isometric vector style with soft shadows, muted material colors (blue-gray, steel gray, sand/ochre), and clear callout lines/labels pointing to parts. The illustrations should have an "engineering diagram" feel: clean contours, a light gradient on metal surfaces, and thin arrows indicating direction/radiation/flux with light yellow/ochre as an accent. Below each illustration there should be a short text block with a bold leading label (e.g. "Shielding:", "Materials:", "Shelters:" in bold) followed by 1–2 sentences in regular sans-serif; certain keywords may be highlighted with warm ochre for emphasis. Iconography and labels should be minimalist and technical (small rectangular label tags above diagram parts), and there should be plenty of whitespace so everything feels like a page from a modern textbook/report. The color palette should be controlled and factual: gray/black text, blue-gray diagrams, and ochre/gold for arrows and highlights; avoid neon, hand-drawn sketch, vintage paper, heavy drop shadows, and chaotic layout — the goal is a clean, print-friendly, technical infographic with three panels, subtle grid, semi-3D diagram illustrations, and clear typographic hierarchy.

title: Style 75
description: For technical subject handouts presented as clean architectural line-drawings.
prompt: This design profile should be infused with an analytical and pedagogical tone that emphasizes precision, structure, and professionalism in every detail. The visual framework rests on a near-white base shade (#F9F9F9) where the dark text (#222222) contrasts with a bright blue accent color (#5B9BD5) to create a clear and methodical aesthetic. The image style is grounded in technical line graphics and isometric diagrams reminiscent of architectural blueprints, complete with dimension markers and schematic flow arrows. The surface should emulate smooth digital paper with razor-sharp vector lines under a flat lighting scheme that guarantees full visibility without any color gradients. The composition builds on a balanced split-screen layout with a vertical flow governed by a strict grid and generous white space to maintain order. Load-bearing structures use thin, precise strokes of 1–2 pixels, while logical connections are illustrated with dashed lines. To guide the viewer, active elements are marked with blue fill, and critical transitions or errors are indicated with red dashed arrows. Typographically, a powerful, bold sans-serif in gothic style or Helvetica is used for the overarching headlines, while body text is kept in a readable medium weight. Technical details and specifications are presented in a condensed sans-serif or a fixed-width typeface, with visual order governed by a clear hierarchy between large main headlines, distinct section dividers, and small, fine-print explanatory notes.

title: Style 76
description: For early-years and primary lessons that need the warmth of a stop-motion claymation world.
prompt: Atmosphere and tonality: The style should feel welcoming and pedagogical with a warm, inspiring demeanor. The mood is whimsical and playful, designed to engage the viewer in a personal way. Visual identity — Background color: #AEE2F6. Text color: #4A3B32. Accent color: #FF8A5B. Complementary palette: #7EC466, #FFD93D, #E86F6F, #F4F1EA, #5DADE2. Imagery and character: The aesthetic is inspired by stop-motion with figures and environments that look as if they were sculpted from modeling clay. The design includes graphic elements such as speech bubbles, arrows for flow charts, and small buttons as decoration. Texture and finish: Surfaces should have a soft, matte clay feel where small physical details like fingerprints are visible. All shapes should have rounded, organic edges to reinforce the impression that everything is physically hand-modeled.

title: Style 77
description: For urban-studies and geography projects with a miniature tilt-shift diorama feel.
prompt: This design vision should convey a sweet and playful mood reminiscent of a miniature garden or a toy world seen from a bird's-eye perspective. The visual expression is built on real photographs of urban or natural landscapes undergoing a transformation in which cars and people appear as tiny plastic models in a diorama. To achieve this surreal effect, a powerful tilt-shift aesthetic is used with a shallow depth-of-field that leaves the top and bottom of the image in heavy blur while the center remains razor-sharp. The color palette draws inspiration from reality but is amplified by giving primary colors a heavily boosted saturation so they act as vivid accents. All text is kept in pure white (#FFFFFF) to create a clear contrast against the colorful environments. The perspective is placed diagonally from above to reinforce the sense of viewing a model, and typographically a rounded sans-serif is used in a style reminiscent of the graphics on a toy package, tying the entire miniaturized aesthetic together.

title: Style 78
description: For pastel affirmation posters and primary-years wellbeing displays.
prompt: This design should be infused with a playful and encouraging tone that feels both soft and decoratively fanciful. The visual foundation rests on a background in a mild pastel pink shade (#FFD1DC) with text in dark gray (#222222) and a purple accent color (#9D84B6), supported by a palette consisting of ice blue (#C3E5F9), light yellow (#FFFACD), lilac (#E0BBE4), coral (#FF9AA2), and mint green (#B5EAD7). The illustration style is marked by soft rainbow-colored gradients, fluffy clouds, and sparkling star elements where cute mascots like unicorns and bears alternate with candy-inspired icons. The textures imitate airbrushed gradients with high-gloss highlights on the icons and smooth vector surfaces, all lit by bright, diffuse lighting that brings out the details through subtle drop shadows. The composition follows a centralized hierarchy that branches from top to bottom into three distinct and rounded pillars, framed by bold pastel-colored borders with soft corners and connected by wide arrows that have a three-dimensional effect. Empty space in the layout is filled with decorative floating hearts, stars, and candies to reinforce the whimsical mood. Typographically, a rounded and poppy sans-serif in balloon style is used for headlines, given a white fill, a thick purple or blue outline, and a soft shadow to really stand out. Body text is set in a friendly rounded sans-serif, such as M PLUS 1c, and placed inside white or light bubbles to ensure the highest possible readability against the colorful, detail-rich background.

title: Style 79
description: For study-skills and note-taking lessons styled as a well-loved personal notebook.
prompt: This design should radiate an authentic and intellectual tone that feels informal, creative, and clearly instructional — like a page from a well-used notebook. The visual identity is built on a cream-colored paper background (#F4F1EA) with horizontal rules, where the primary text in dark blue (#0E2A5C) and a red accent color (#D12828) create a lively dynamic alongside sand-toned elements (#C8AB83). The image style is characterized by hand-drawn flow charts, a spiral binding with discreet shadows in the left margin, and realistic traces of use such as coffee stains and ink bleeding into the fibers of the matte paper. The composition proceeds top-to-bottom with loose, organic groupings of stick figures, speech bubbles, and arrows, where generous empty areas are used for additional marginalia and emphasizing stars. Lighting mimics a flat and clear natural ambient light, as from a desktop scan, which accentuates the varying line weight of a gel pen with natural jitter. Typographically, a bold marker style is used for headlines while body text consists of a relaxed mix of cursive and printed handwriting, with certain parts framed or underlined in red ink and complemented by Japanese kanji and kana characters integrated among the other symbols.

title: Style 80
description: For home-ec, chemistry, or process-lesson handouts told in six warm recipe steps.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "step-by-step recipe infographic" poster in warm, modern kitchen illustration style with a clean white/beige background and a soft warm gradient (cream, sand, light caramel) in each step panel. At the top, a large headline in a clear serif or strong sans-serif (printed, editorial feel) should be centered, followed by a 2x3 grid of six equally sized step cards with rounded corners, even margins, and a light drop shadow. Each step card should have a large step title in uppercase (e.g. "1. …") with a short headline, a central 2D/semi-flat illustration of kitchen tools/ingredients/hand motion in a clean vector-like style with soft shadows and subtle gradients (not photorealistic), plus 1–2 short lines of explanatory text at the bottom of the card in readable sans-serif; keep the layout consistent and scannable. Connect the steps with clear dark-brown arrows between the cards in a "snake" sequence (top row left → right, then down and back right → left or following clear flow logic), with uniform arrow styling and rounded tips. The color palette should be warm and appetizing: beige/cream as the base, chocolate brown for text and arrows, plus soft accents in coffee/cocoa/caramel tones and muted blue-gray for metal/glass; illustrated objects should have clean contours, light cel-shading, and soft highlights that give a "friendly cookbook" feel. At the bottom add a discreet footnote line in smaller text, centered, as a small "Note:" in italic or lighter weight. Avoid neon, dark HUD, vintage grunge, hand-drawn sketch, and exaggerated 3D; the goal is a clean, modern, print-friendly cooking infographic with six panels, consistent icon illustration, warm neutral background, and clear arrows that guide the eye step by step.

title: Style 81
description: For policy briefs and civics lessons where a watercolour map anchors the discussion.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "policy/editorial infographic" in modern report/think-tank style with a warm off-white/paper-toned background and subtle texture (light grain, soft blotches), where the visual centerpiece is a large central map illustration with watercolor/gouache feel in muted earth tones and soft gradient transitions. At the top, a large, centered title in dark blue-gray serif or clear semi-serif (report feel) should sit with plenty of air around it, and below it the layout should be divided into three clear zones: a left column with 3–4 "stat blocks" (large numbers in strong sans-serif, with a headline in uppercase and 1–2 lines of explanatory text), the middle with the map and a few discreet markers (pins/circles) plus symbolic overlays (e.g. vortex/iconic natural phenomena) integrated into the map's painting, and a right column with 2–3 "impact/strategy blocks" that combine large percentages, small icon pairs, and short text passages. The icon style should be consistent and flat with light rounding, in muted colors matching the map's palette (teal, sand, terracotta, dark blue-gray), and each icon should have a simple shape and clean contour without neon. The map should have an illustrated, semi-artistic finish: watercolor layers, soft edges, subtle brush strokes, and tonal fields that separate regions, with thin white/gray lines for borders and a few small silhouette icons (houses, trees, water) that are stylized and integrated as "data storytelling". The layout should feel well-edited and balanced: clear margins, consistent left alignment in the text columns, plenty of whitespace, and a limited color palette where large numbers/percentages use a distinct accent color (teal/blue) for hierarchy. Add discreet section headings above the left and right columns (uppercase, small but clear) and use thin dividing lines or very subtle block backgrounds to group information, without becoming "card UI". The overall impression should be a serious, modern, print-friendly infographic that feels like a page from a policy report: central watercolor map + flanked by stat boxes with large numbers and simple icons, no strong gradients, no hand-drawn sketchnotes, no neon/HUD effects, and no photorealism.

title: Style 82
description: For symmetric design-theory or philosophy handouts with a meditative centerpiece.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal premium design explainer infographic with a warm white/ivory background and plenty of whitespace. The style should be modern and polished: clean vector + soft gradient/soft-3D (not photorealistic). Composition: At the top, a large centered title in bold sans-serif. Place a thin rounded rectangular frame around the main area. Let decorative, thin "flow lines" in matching accent colors weave along the frame and corners. In the middle: a large, vertical hero illustration that feels symmetrical and iconic. It should have soft gradients and depth, and serve as the anchor for the entire layout. Place 5–7 callout blocks around the hero (left and right). Each block has: a headline (bold), 1–2 sentences of description, and a thin connector line pointing in toward the hero. Imagery: Use colorful, sweeping fluid ribbons/blobs that flow from the hero and tie the callouts together. Each callout should have a small icon/mini-illustration in the same style (e.g. typographic letters, grid/pixels, brush strokes, image icon, symbols), but keep them generic and design-system-like. Mix geometric elements (grid, letters, icons) with organic elements (ribbons, blobs, brush strokes). Color and light: Background: warm white/ivory. Accents: rainbow gradient with cyan/blue/purple/magenta/red/orange/yellow/green. Soft shadows and subtle highlights; no hard contours. Typography: Sans-serif throughout, clear hierarchy, lots of air. Headlines clear and short; body text short and easy to read. Finish: Subtle, almost imperceptible paper-grain texture in the background. Clean, modern, "brand style guide" feel. Negative instructions: Avoid dark background, neon HUD, vintage blueprint, grunge, photorealism, heavy frames, overly long text blocks, flat one-color iconography without gradients, hard black outlines.

title: Style 83
description: For mid-century retro posters — reading weeks, cafeteria themes, or vintage history units.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal retro infographic in 1950s "mid-century pop culture poster" style with a distinct print feel: warm beige, lightly blotched paper background with subtle grain, halftone/print texture, and faint color fading at the edges. Use a playful, hand-lettered/comic display typography for the main headline (thick letters with dark outline and a light shadow) and place it in a large, rounded banner header at the very top with a pastel gradient (turquoise/teal) and a contrasting secondary banner beneath in pink, both with "chrome"-like highlight lines and thick outlines that feel like 1950s sign painting. The main layout should consist of three large, equally wide cards/panels side by side (left–middle–right), each panel with heavily rounded corners, multi-layered frames (mint/teal/pink), and a retro "inset" feel with inner shadow, like plastic/chrome trim. Inside each panel there should be a large central icon/illustration in clean 2D retro-cartoon style with soft gradients and clear outlines (sticker feel), plus short text blocks in bullet form with small pictograms/emoji-like icons as bullets; keep the text scannable and neatly aligned with clear headings in a bold retro display style. Add small decorative "atomic age" ornaments around the background: starbursts, boomerang shapes, small sparkles, and confetti dots in pastel (mint, light yellow, coral, light blue), plus some small signage-like detail (e.g. a neon badge) in the same style. The color palette should be consistently 1950s: turquoise/teal, mint green, soft pink, coral red, butter yellow, and muted black for outlines; use subtle highlights that imitate chrome and plastic, but avoid photorealism and 3D render. The overall impression should be a nostalgic, cheerful poster infographic with clear frames, central illustrations, retro typography, atomic-age decorations, and printed paper finish; avoid modern minimalist corporate style, neon HUD, thin line charts, and overly clean vector lines without texture.

title: Style 84
description: For strategy briefs contrasting old and new ways of working in schools.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal modern "tech strategy explainer infographic" in clean semi-flat vector illustration style with soft gradients, clear contours, and a light 3D feel on objects, on a light warm white background with subtle paper grain. The layout should be clearly divided into two contrasting halves (left vs right) with a diagonal or vertical dividing line and a consistent visual narrative: the left side should feel fast, light, and dynamic with a wide, curving path in color gradient (turquoise → orange) and a futuristic car/rocket-like metaphor moving forward, while the right side should feel heavy, gray, and bureaucratic with a slow machine/convoy of industrial vehicles in muted gray tones, papers flying, and stop signs/obstacles along a sluggish path. Use many small icon-based callouts with thin leader lines: short headlines in bold sans-serif, brief 1–2 lines of microcopy, and pictograms (clock, rocket, shield, gear, document, stop sign, hourglass, email/chat bubbles) in the same style, placed around the main scenes. At the top, a large centered title in dark sans-serif with a smaller subheading line beneath, and at the bottom center a compact comparison box/mini-chart (a small table or bar-like comparison) with icons and short labels, all in the same clean infographic aesthetic. The color palette should be controlled and thematically coded: the left half dominated by bright blue/teal and warm orange accents with motion lines, the right half dominated by cool gray/blue with muted accents and more "paper clutter", but both should share the same typography and line style for a unified poster feel. Add dynamic motion effects on the left side (speed lines, arrows, small rockets) and visual friction symbols on the right side (stop signs, barriers, stacked documents, speech-bubble clusters), but keep everything tidy, scannable, and print-friendly. Avoid photorealism, hand-drawn sketchnote, neon HUD, and heavy grunge; the goal is a professional, narrative "concept infographic" with two sides, strong metaphor illustration, clear callouts, and modern semi-flat vector finish.

title: Style 85
description: For history timelines where four painted epoch scenes carry the lesson.
prompt: Create a wide, horizontal "cinematic illustrated timeline poster" in premium fantasy/history explainer style with four clearly separated era panels side by side, like a curated museum exhibition. At the top, a large, elegant title in classic serif should be centered, with a smaller subtitle above or below in the same typographic family; use light warm-white text with a subtle shadow against a dark, atmospheric background. Each era panel should be a fully illustrated scene in digitally painted concept-art style (semi-realistic, high detail, soft brushstrokes, clear materials, cinematic lighting) with its own color temperature and atmosphere: panel 1 misty, earthy, and raw (cold green-gray forests/mud), panel 2 rich and ornamented (warm gold/red tones, medieval manuscript/illumination feel and a decorative frame like an illuminated book-page border), panel 3 more realistic and "historical-romantic" (neutral daylight colors, Victorian/early-modern interior details, books, instruments), panel 4 strongly neon-cinematic and modern (purple/blue cyber glow, sci-fi city silhouette, high contrast).
